title: The Fifth Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
February 7, 2021 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fifth Sunday after the Epiphany in the traditional church calendar, falling within the Season after the Epiphany (Ordinary Time). The Revised Common Lectionary for this date likely included Isaiah 40:21-31 (God's power and renewal of strength), Psalm 147:1-11, 20c, 1 Corinthians 9:16-23 (Paul on becoming all things to all people), and Mark 1:29-39 (Jesus healing Simon's mother-in-law and the sick). The liturgical color would be green, signifying Ordinary Time and growth in faith.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early months following General Convention 2021 resolutions passed in July 2020, including actions on racial justice and pandemic relief. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Glasspool (who had served since 2017), was engaged in ongoing diocesan recovery and community rebuilding efforts in the post-pandemic period. Saint John's Cathedral, as the seat of the bishop, would have been central to diocesan communications and liturgical leadership during this time of institutional navigation.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing severe winter weather in early February 2021, with significant snow expected mid-week (around February 10-11). Colorado's COVID-19 case rates remained elevated but were beginning to show modest declines from the January 2021 surge. Local news was focused on vaccine distribution challenges across the state, with Colorado healthcare systems still in early phases of the rollout and significant demand exceeding supply.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The second impeachment trial of President Donald Trump had just begun in the Senate on February 9, 2021 (two days after this sermon), dominating national political coverage. The United States was in the midst of the winter COVID-19 surge with over 400,000 confirmed deaths; vaccine distribution was widely criticized as chaotic and inequitable across states. The economy remained volatile with discussions of stimulus checks and unemployment benefits as pandemic relief measures debated in Congress.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The military coup in Myanmar (Burma) on February 1, 2021, was the dominant international story, with international outcry over the detention of elected leaders including Aung San Suu Kyi. The COVID-19 pandemic continued globally with variants of concern emerging, particularly the Alpha variant spreading in the UK and Europe. Brazil was experiencing severe drought and a health crisis with its COVID-19 death toll rapidly rising.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of exhaustion and division: one year into the pandemic, Americans were weary from isolation, economic disruption, and political polarization. Super Bowl LV would air five days later (February 7, 2021 was game day), offering a rare moment of collective distraction for many Americans. Despite vaccine hope on the horizon, there was widespread anxiety about when normalcy might return and deep uncertainty about the immediate future.
